note: Community guide catalog. Ratings/reviews here are curated seeds; your own ratings and AI-moderated reviews are stored on your device and can be exported to contribute.

meta.title: Replace AC Compressor and ECT2
meta.subtitle: A/C compressor + condenser, ECT2 sensor, HG-1

reviews.author: wrenchingdad
reviews.text: The 'cap every open line instantly' warning saved me — did all three jobs on one coolant drain. Clear and safety-first.

reviews.author: civicsi_owner
reviews.text: Great detail on the A/C oil being R-1234yf specific. Wished the ECT2 socket size was nailed down, but the 'measure the flats' note is honest.

guide.title: 2015–2019 Subaru Outback 2.5i — Front Brakes
guide.subtitle: Front pads + rotors
guide.safety: Chock the rear wheels and use jack stands — never rely on the jack alone. Never let a hanging caliper dangle by its hose.

steps.t: Loosen lugs, lift, remove the wheel

items: Crack the **lug nuts** 1/2 turn while the car is on the ground.
Lift the front and set it on **jack stands**; chock the rears.
Remove the wheel.

body.title: Stands, not the jack
body.text: A jack can drop without warning. Use rated stands on level ground.

steps.t: Remove caliper, hang it, swap pads & rotor

items: Remove the two **caliper slide bolts**; lift the caliper off.
**Hang the caliper** from the spring with a hook or zip tie — do not stress the hose.
Remove the two caliper-bracket bolts, then the bracket.
Pull the old rotor (a **retaining screw** or a rust bond may hold it).
Fit the new rotor, bracket, and pads.

body.title: Compress the piston
body.text: Push the caliper piston back with a C-clamp or piston tool before the caliper goes over the new, thicker pads. Watch the brake-fluid reservoir level as it rises.
Caliper bracket bolts ~89 ft-lb; slide/guide bolts ~27 ft-lb.
body.verify: Verify against the factory service manual for your exact VIN.

steps.t: Before you drive

items: Both sides done; hardware torqued to spec
**Pump the brake pedal** until firm before moving
Lug nuts torqued in a star pattern
No leaks; wheels back on; hubcaps seated

body.text: Lug nuts ~89 ft-lb.
body.verify: Confirm the pattern and torque in the factory manual for your VIN.

body.title: Bed the brakes
body.text: Do several moderate 30→10 mph slowdowns (no full stops) to bed the pads, then let them cool.

tools.d: Caliper guide bolts are often Torx/hex

reviews.author: boxer_wagon
reviews.text: Did my '17 Outback in an afternoon. The piston-compression reminder and the bed-in step are the parts people skip.

reviews.author: newdiy
reviews.text: Straightforward. Add a note to check the guide-pin boots while you're in there.

guide.title: Engine Oil & Filter Change — Universal
guide.subtitle: Drain, replace filter, refill to spec
guide.safety: Work on a cool-to-warm engine (not scalding hot). Support the vehicle on jack stands if you go underneath — never just the jack.

steps.t: Warm briefly, park level, gather

items: Run the engine 2–3 minutes so the oil flows, then shut off.
Park **level**; if lifting, use **jack stands**.
Confirm your **oil type/viscosity and capacity** and the correct **filter** for your VIN.

body.text: Capacity and viscosity vary by engine.
body.verify: Look up the exact oil spec and capacity for your VIN before buying.

steps.t: Drain oil, replace filter, reinstall plug

items: Position the **drain pan**, remove the drain plug, let it fully drain.
Remove the **oil filter** (spin-off or cartridge); oil a new gasket/O-ring and install.
Reinstall the drain plug with a **new crush washer**; snug — do not overtighten.
Torque the plug and filter to spec.

body.title: New crush washer
body.text: Reusing a flattened washer is a common slow-leak. Use a fresh one.
Drain plug torque is typically ~25–30 ft-lb (varies).
body.verify: Use the plug torque for your engine from the service manual.

steps.t: Refill, check level, leak-check

items: Correct oil type & capacity added
Run 30s, shut off, wait, check the **dipstick** and top to the full mark
No leaks at the plug or filter
Reset the **oil-life monitor** if equipped

body.title: Dispose responsibly
body.text: Take the used oil and filter to an auto-parts store or recycling center — most take it free.

items.q: engine oil filter for my vehicle
oil drain plug crush washer assortment

reviews.author: firsttimer
reviews.text: Clear enough that I did my first oil change with zero drama. The crush-washer warning is gold.

guide.title: Roadside Safety First — 20 Vehicle Set
guide.subtitle: Stop, position, decide: fix, limp, tow, or call 911
guide.safety: Your first repair is getting out of danger. Do not work in a live lane, on a blind curve, on soft ground, or beside fast traffic. Injury, fire, crash damage, fuel smell, smoke, brake failure, steering failure, oil-pressure warning, or unsafe shoulder means call emergency help or a tow.

steps.t: Move out of traffic before diagnosing

items: Signal early, slow smoothly, and aim for the widest shoulder, parking lot, side street, or ramp gore you can reach safely.
Turn on **hazard lights**, set the parking brake, and keep passengers away from traffic.
Exit on the passenger side when the driver door opens toward traffic. If you cannot exit safely, stay belted and call for help.

body.title: No shoulder, no repair
body.text: A narrow shoulder on a fast road is not a work area. Call roadside assistance or 911 depending on the danger.

steps.t: Pick the visibility tools first

items: Proper tool: reflective triangles, LED flare, high-visibility vest, gloves, and a headlamp.
Likely-carried tool: hazard lights, phone flashlight, bright clothing, a passenger with a phone, and the vehicle manual.
No-go if: traffic-side work is required, the vehicle is unstable, weather makes you hard to see, or you feel rushed by passing traffic.

body.title: Use the phone early
body.text: Share your location before starting. If the repair fails, help is already in motion.

steps.t: Choose the safest next move

items: Tow: crash damage, fire/smoke, fuel smell, brake or steering loss, red oil-pressure warning, severe overheating, wheel damage, sidewall tire damage, or no safe workspace.
Roadside fix: low tire, dead 12V battery, blown accessory fuse, loose splash shield, washer/wiper issue, or mild coolant top-off with the engine cooled.
Driveway follow-up: recheck torque, pressure, coolant level, battery terminals, fuse cause, and temporary repairs before normal driving.

tools.d: Headlamp or work light for night work

items.n: Printed owner manual or saved PDF
items.d: Jack points, fuse maps, tow limits

guide.title: Low Tire or Slow Tread Leak — Inflate, Seal, or Plug
guide.subtitle: Applies to the 20 selected 2012–2022 vehicles
guide.safety: Only work where the vehicle is fully out of traffic and parked on firm, level ground. Do not repair sidewall damage, shoulder damage, a shredded tire, a bent wheel, or a tire driven flat long enough to overheat.

steps.t: Confirm it is a slow tread leak

items: Read the cold PSI target on the driver-door placard, not the tire sidewall maximum.
Check all four tires and the spare if equipped.
Look for a nail or screw in the tread area, a leaking valve stem, bead leak, wheel damage, sidewall bubble, or split sidewall.

body.title: Sidewall damage means stop
body.text: Do not plug or seal a sidewall. Install the spare or call for a tow.

steps.t: Use the best tire tool available

items: Proper tool: tire pressure gauge, 12V inflator, tread-only plug kit with reamer/plug tool, soapy water, pliers, and valve-core tool.
Likely-carried tool: OEM inflator/sealant kit, gas-station air pump, phone flashlight, gloves, and the door-jamb placard.
No-go if: the tire loses air quickly, the puncture is larger than about 1/4 inch, the wheel is bent, or the car must be jacked on an unsafe shoulder.

body.title: Sealant is temporary
body.text: OEM sealant can damage TPMS sensors and is meant to get you to a tire shop, not to avoid a proper repair.

steps.t: Get to a safe repair location

items: Inflate to the placard PSI and wait a few minutes.
If pressure holds, drive slowly to a tire shop or home and recheck pressure.
If using a plug kit, plug only a small tread puncture and trim the plug flush after reinflating.
If pressure falls again, stop and use the spare or tow.
Pressure matches door placard
TPMS warning understood and monitored
No sidewall or shoulder repair attempted
Tire shop follow-up planned for internal patch/plug inspection

tools.d: Pull nail or screw from tread

items.n: Tire plugs or OEM sealant cartridge
items.q: tire repair plugs OEM tire sealant

guide.title: Flat Tire Spare Install — Cars, Crossovers, and SUVs
guide.subtitle: Equinox, Malibu, Traverse, Journey, Edge, Escape, Explorer, Focus, Fusion, Terrain, Sonata, Grand Cherokee, Wrangler, Optima, Sorento, Altima, Rogue
guide.safety: Change a tire only on firm, level ground away from traffic. Never crawl under a vehicle supported by the factory jack. If the flat is on the traffic side or the shoulder is narrow, call for roadside assistance.

steps.t: Find the spare, jack, lug wrench, and lock key

items: Check the cargo floor, side compartment, tailgate area, or rear-mounted spare location.
Find the jack point in the owner manual or stamped pinch-weld mark.
Set the parking brake and chock a wheel opposite the flat if you have a chock or a solid improvised block.

body.title: Some trims have no spare
body.text: If the vehicle has only an inflator/sealant kit and the tire is shredded, sidewall-damaged, or off the bead, call for a tow.

steps.t: Use the safest lifting tools available

items: Proper tool: factory jack at the correct jack point, wheel chocks, breaker bar, torque wrench, wheel lock key, gloves, and reflective marker.
Likely-carried tool: OEM scissor jack, OEM lug wrench, spare-tire kit, floor mat as a kneeling pad, phone flashlight, and a passenger watching traffic.
No-go if: ground is soft, sloped, icy, narrow, or the wheel cannot be safely removed without entering traffic.

body.text: Lug-nut torque varies by vehicle and wheel.
body.verify: Use the owner manual or service data for the exact VIN; temporary roadside tightening must be rechecked with a torque wrench.

steps.t: Install the spare and limit speed

items: Loosen lug nuts 1/2 turn while the tire is on the ground.
Jack only at the approved point until the tire clears the ground.
Install the spare, hand-start all lug nuts, snug in a star pattern, lower the vehicle, then tighten again in a star pattern.
Stow the flat, tools, and jack before driving.
All lugs hand-started without cross-threading
Jack removed and tools stowed
Temporary spare speed and distance limits followed
Lug torque and tire pressure rechecked at home or shop

tools.d: Driveway proper support; roadside factory jack only for wheel change
Break stuck lug nuts loose in driveway
Final lug torque at home or shop

tools.q: vehicle factory jack lug wrench kit

tools.d: Required if locking lug nuts are installed

items.n: Inflated spare tire or tire repair kit

guide.title: Flat Tire Spare Install — Underbody Pickup Spare
guide.subtitle: Silverado 1500, F-150, and Ram 1500 roadside spare workflow
guide.safety: Pickup spares are heavy and often stored under the bed. Work only on firm, level ground away from traffic. Do not put any part of your body under the truck while it is supported only by the jack.

steps.t: Assemble the spare-lowering kit

items: Find the jack, lug wrench, spare crank rods, wheel lock key if equipped, and the spare access hole.
Use the owner manual diagram; pickup crank rods often assemble in a specific order.
Lower the spare fully, slide it out, and confirm it is inflated and not rust-stuck or cable-damaged.

body.title: Do not improvise the hoist drive
body.text: If the spare hoist, key, or cable is seized, roadside force can strand the truck worse. Call assistance.

steps.t: Use pickup-rated lifting and lug tools

items: Proper tool: OEM spare-lowering kit, rated bottle/factory jack at the correct axle/frame point, wheel chocks, breaker bar, torque wrench, gloves, and kneeling pad.
Likely-carried tool: factory jack, crank rods, OEM lug wrench, hitch-area access tools, phone flashlight, and work gloves.
No-go if: loaded trailer, unstable cargo, soft shoulder, seized spare hoist, missing lock key, or traffic-side work.

body.text: Pickup lug torque is high and varies by truck and wheel.
body.verify: Use the exact owner manual torque and recheck with a torque wrench as soon as practical.

steps.t: Mount the spare and secure the flat

items: Break lug nuts loose before lifting.
Lift at the approved point; keep the truck as low as possible while clearing the tire.
Hand-start all lug nuts, snug in a star pattern, lower, then tighten in stages.
Secure the flat tire in the bed or underbody carrier only if the hoist and cable are working correctly.
Spare seated flat on hub
All lugs snugged in pattern
Flat tire and tools secured
Trailer towing paused until tire, torque, and load rating are verified

tools.q: F-150 Silverado Ram spare tire tool kit

items.q: pickup spare tire lock key replacement

guide.title: Dead 12V Battery — Jump, Terminal Check, and Driveway Follow-up
guide.subtitle: Applies to all 20 selected vehicles
guide.safety: Do not jump a frozen, cracked, leaking, swollen, or rotten-egg-smelling battery. Keep sparks away from the battery. Hybrid and start-stop vehicles may have special jump posts; use the owner manual.

steps.t: Separate battery failure from other no-starts

items: Listen for rapid clicking, weak dash lights, or no crank after lights were left on.
Check that the gear selector is in Park or Neutral and the brake pedal is pressed.
Inspect battery terminals for looseness, heavy corrosion, or a clamp that rotates by hand.

body.title: Tow instead
body.text: Tow or get professional help if there is smoke, melted wiring, fuel smell, crash damage, or repeated stalling after a jump.

steps.t: Jump with the safest available gear

items: Proper tool: lithium jump pack with reverse-polarity protection, safety glasses, 10mm wrench, terminal brush, and multimeter.
Likely-carried tool: jumper cables and a helper vehicle, adjustable wrench, bottled water for light corrosion cleanup, and phone flashlight.
No-go if: terminals are damaged, battery case is compromised, cables get hot, or the vehicle dies again immediately after removing the jump source.

body.title: Use the marked jump points
body.text: Many vehicles route negative clamps to a remote ground point. Follow the owner manual instead of guessing at hidden batteries.

steps.t: Start, remove clamps, and plan the real fix

items: Connect positive to positive, then negative to the specified ground point.
Start the disabled vehicle and remove clamps in reverse order without letting clamps touch.
Let the vehicle run long enough to reach a safer place, then test battery and charging voltage in the driveway or at a parts store.
Correct polarity used
Terminals tight enough that they do not rotate
Charging/battery test planned
Radio/window/idle relearn handled if the vehicle requires it

guide.title: Blown Fuse Quick Check — Lights, Wipers, Outlet, Trailer
guide.subtitle: Safe roadside electrical triage for all 20 selected vehicles
guide.safety: Replace a fuse only with the same amp rating. If the replacement blows immediately, stop. A blown fuse is a symptom; repeatedly replacing it can overheat wiring.

steps.t: Find the correct fuse box and label

items: Use the owner manual, fuse-box cover, or phone photo of the fuse map.
Target only low-risk accessories such as power outlet, washer pump, wipers, horn, lamps, or trailer lights.
Turn the vehicle off before pulling a fuse unless the manual says otherwise.

body.title: Do not bypass a fuse
body.text: Foil, wire, oversized fuses, and jumper hacks can start a fire.

steps.t: Pull and test without damaging the panel

items: Proper tool: fuse puller, same-amp mini/low-profile fuse assortment, test light or multimeter, and owner manual.
Likely-carried tool: spare fuse from the panel, needle-nose pliers, phone camera zoom, and the fuse-box cover diagram.
No-go if: the fuse blows again, there is melted plastic, a burning smell, wet electronics, or the circuit is safety-critical and still dead.

body.title: One spare only
body.text: A single same-amp replacement is a diagnostic step. A second blown fuse means the fault is still present.

steps.t: Restore the circuit or stop safely

items: Compare the suspect fuse to a known-good same-size fuse or test continuity.
Install only the same amp rating.
Operate the circuit once while watching for immediate failure.
If it fails again, leave it off and plan a wiring diagnosis.
Same amp rating installed
Fuse seated fully
Fuse-box cover reinstalled
Cause recorded for driveway diagnosis

tools.q: mini low profile automotive fuse assortment

guide.title: Visibility Roadside Fixes — Wipers, Washer Fluid, and Bulbs
guide.subtitle: Get safe visibility back before continuing
guide.safety: Poor visibility is a stop-now condition in rain, snow, fog, or darkness. Do not continue at highway speed with failed wipers, empty washer fluid during road spray, or missing required lights at night.

steps.t: Separate fluid, blade, fuse, and bulb faults

items: If the windshield is dirty and the pump runs, add washer fluid.
If the blade smears, chatters, splits, or leaves an arc, replace the blade.
If wipers or washer pump are dead, check the related fuse only if the location is safe.
If a bulb is out, replace only an accessible bulb you can reach without removing major parts roadside.

body.title: Night and rain change the decision
body.text: If you cannot see or cannot be seen, stop somewhere safe and fix it, wait it out, or tow.

steps.t: Use a simple visibility kit

items: Proper tool: correct-length wiper blades, washer fluid rated for the temperature, bulb gloves, correct bulb, trim tool, and screwdriver set.
Likely-carried tool: gas-station universal blade, gas-station washer fluid, paper towel, spare fuse, phone flashlight, and gloves.
No-go if: the wiper arm spring is broken, linkage is loose, headlight housing must be removed beside traffic, or wiring is melted.

body.title: Do not touch halogen glass
body.text: Skin oil can shorten bulb life. Use gloves or a clean towel.

steps.t: Verify visibility before driving

items: Fill washer fluid and run the spray briefly.
Install the blade until the latch clicks, then gently lower the arm onto the glass.
Replace accessible bulbs with the correct part number and confirm function.
Clean glass, mirrors, lights, and camera lenses before leaving.
Wipers sweep without metal-on-glass contact
Washer fluid sprays both sides
Headlights, brake lights, turn signals, and hazards checked
Temporary bulb or blade fix replaced with correct part if needed

tools.d: Best fix for smearing or split rubber

items.q: vehicle headlight brake turn signal bulb

guide.title: Mild Overheat or Low Coolant — Roadside Triage Only
guide.subtitle: Cool down, top off if safe, then get to a shop or driveway diagnosis
guide.safety: Never open a hot radiator or pressurized coolant cap. Steam, red temperature warning, fast coolant loss, oil/coolant mixing, belt failure, or repeated overheating means tow.

steps.t: Stop heat damage before adding fluid

items: Turn off A/C, turn heat to hot if you must limp a short distance to safety, then park and shut down.
Open the hood from the cabin release and wait at least 30 minutes before touching caps or hoses.
Look from a distance for steam, coolant pouring out, broken belt, or electric fan damage.

body.title: Hot coolant can blind or burn
body.text: Do not loosen a hot cap. Waiting is part of the repair.

steps.t: Top off only when the system is cool

items: Proper tool: correct premixed coolant, spill-free funnel, gloves, rags, flashlight, and OBD2 scanner for temperature data.
Likely-carried tool: bottled water as a temporary top-off, towel, phone flashlight, and heater controls.
No-go if: temperature warning is red, coolant pours out, there is steam, oil pressure warning appears, or the engine knocks.

body.title: Water is only a get-safe measure
body.text: Plain water reduces freeze and corrosion protection. Replace with the correct coolant mix after the emergency.

steps.t: Verify it stays cool before moving

items: When cool, check the overflow bottle first; add premix to the marked level if low.
If the system has a safe degas bottle cap and is fully cool, top off slowly.
Restart and watch the gauge for a few minutes before moving.
Drive only to a nearby safe location if the gauge stays normal.
Cap left closed until cool
Coolant level visible in reservoir
Gauge stable before moving
Driveway leak check or shop visit planned

items.d: Match vehicle spec and color chemistry

guide.title: Dragging Splash Shield or Loose Trim — Temporary Roadside Secure
guide.subtitle: Stop scraping without crawling under the vehicle
guide.safety: Do not crawl under a vehicle at the roadside. Do not reach near hot exhaust, moving belts, rotating fans, or traffic. If the loose part contacts a tire, brake line, fuel line, or exhaust, tow.

steps.t: Identify what is dragging

items: Park safely, set the brake, and let hot parts cool.
Look from the side, front, or wheel well; use the phone camera rather than putting your head under the vehicle.
Confirm the loose item is plastic splash shield, fender liner, bumper trim, or heat shield edge, not a brake, fuel, steering, suspension, or exhaust part.

body.title: Structural or fluid parts are not trim
body.text: If a line, cable, metal arm, exhaust hanger, or wheel-area part is loose, stop and tow.

steps.t: Secure it for a short limp only

items: Proper tool: trim-clip assortment, trim tools, zip ties, side cutters, screwdriver set, gloves, and work light.
Likely-carried tool: duct tape, zip ties, shoelace, pocket knife, phone flashlight, and gloves.
No-go if: the part can touch a tire, driveshaft, axle, belt, fan, hot exhaust, or the repair requires getting under the vehicle.

body.title: Tape is not enough near heat
body.text: Tape can melt or let go. Keep temporary supports away from exhaust and moving parts.

steps.t: Tie upward, then inspect after a short roll

items: Use existing holes or solid plastic edges to zip-tie the loose panel upward.
Trim zip-tie tails and verify nothing can rub the tire at full steering lock.
Drive slowly around a parking lot, stop, and recheck.
Replace broken clips or damaged panels in the driveway.
No part contacts tire, brake, belt, fan, axle, or exhaust
Temporary tie cannot drag loose
Noise gone at low speed
Permanent clips or panel repair planned

tools.d: Pull damaged clips and tension zip ties

tools.q: heavy duty zip ties flush cutters

guide.title: Light Traction Recovery — Snow, Mud, Sand, or Grass
guide.subtitle: SUVs, trucks, and crossovers in the 20-vehicle set
guide.safety: This is for low-speed self-recovery only. Do not stand near spinning tires or tow straps. Do not use non-rated bumper, hitch ball, suspension, or tie-down points for pulling.

steps.t: Prevent the vehicle from sinking deeper

items: Stop spinning tires as soon as forward motion stops.
Clear snow, mud, sand, or grass from in front of the drive tires and under low-hanging panels.
Turn traction control to the mode recommended by the owner manual for snow, sand, mud, or low-speed rocking.

body.title: No strap heroics
body.text: Recovery straps and kinetic ropes require rated recovery points and training. A hitch ball can become a projectile.

steps.t: Add traction without damaging the vehicle

items: Proper tool: traction boards, shovel, tire gauge, gloves, and rated recovery point knowledge from the owner manual.
Likely-carried tool: floor mats as a last-resort traction surface, cardboard, sand or kitty litter, small shovel, and phone flashlight.
No-go if: water is rising, the vehicle is high-centered, a wheel is hanging, traffic is close, or a pull requires unrated attachment points.

body.title: Floor mats are sacrificial
body.text: They can shoot out or be destroyed. Keep people clear and use them only to get to a safer surface.

steps.t: Rock gently and stop if it worsens

items: Place traction material tight against the drive tires in the direction of travel.
Use light throttle only; rocking between reverse and drive should be gentle and brief.
Once moving, keep momentum to firm ground without hard steering or high speed.
Recheck for damaged splash shields, tires, and warning lights.
No one stood in front of or behind spinning tires
Vehicle reached firm ground
Undercarriage and tires checked
Professional recovery called if the first careful attempts failed

tools.q: vehicle traction boards snow mud sand
